coverage.rationale: T1 is explicitly constructed as the no-new-policy passive counterfactual, describing drift in identification, capacity, and demographic trends under unchanged rules, exactly as bounded by S1.

T2 is bounded strictly to reconciling statistical series and empirically disentangling prevalence, diagnostic-practice, access, and incentive drivers, matching S2's evidence/monitoring-architecture boundary without touching service or placement rules.

T3 addresses SNI/BTMN category boundaries, expert-committee gatekeeping, waiting lists, and tiered/non-categorical alternatives using transferability-tested comparative reform evidence (Wales, Finland, Poland), matching S3's definitional/adjudication boundary.

T4 is bounded to per-pupil weighting, normative multipliers, and census- versus diagnosis-gated allocation as incentive mechanisms, explicitly excluding service-delivery capacity, matching S4's funding-design boundary.

T5 addresses gyógypedagógus shortages, regional maldistribution, co-teaching infrastructure gaps, and the assess-to-serve implementation gap, bounded to workforce and delivery capacity without touching identification rules or finance formulas, matching S5.

T6 confronts the interaction of SEN classification, free school choice, institutional admissions discretion, and Roma over-classification in reshaping school composition, including outstanding legal-accountability obligations, matching S6's compositional-sorting and anti-segregation boundary.

S7's adjacent-domain coordination content is distributed rather than given a standalone proposal: T2 explicitly uses the 2018/2020 school-readiness rule change as a driver-attribution test case for adjacent-policy effects on identification, while T5's workforce capacity mechanisms address the rural school-network and small-single-class-school structural constraints (F-live-demography-08) that interact with SEN service delivery; together they retain S7's substantive content on adjacent-lever coordination without duplicating a dedicated new proposal, since S7 is explicitly bounded to coordinating levers already addressed elsewhere rather than introducing SEN-specific rule changes.

assumptions: Current rules and funding formulas remain formally unchanged through the projection horizon
Demographic projections (birth decline, cohort shrinkage) are treated as exogenous and largely locked in
proposals.boundary: Bounded to describing projected drift and its distributional consequences under current rules; does not propose any rule, funding, capacity, or structural change.
proposals.change_lever: None — this is an explicit no-action counterfactual documenting what continues if existing rules, funding formulas, and gatekeeping structures are left in place.
costs: Continued unresolved legal exposure under ECtHR and EU infringement procedures (F-live-political_feasibility-06, F-live-political_feasibility-09)
Continued workforce shortages concentrated in Budapest and rural/northeastern regions with no corrective mechanism (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05, F-live-school_network_planning-05)
Continued fiscal squeeze on local school financing compounding with demographic decline (F-live-demography-09)
proposals.equity_impact: Absent intervention, existing evidence indicates Roma over-classification, segregation, and unidentified need among multiply disadvantaged families are likely to persist or worsen, since these dynamics are already documented under current rules (F-live-school_network_planning-09, F-live-legal_and_governance-10, F-live-hungarian_education_system-09).
expected_benefits: Provides a transparent, low-cost comparator against which the costs and benefits of active reform proposals (T2-T6) can be measured
Avoids premature or poorly evidenced intervention where verification (T2) has not yet established drivers
proposals.goal: Describe the counterfactual trajectory of SEN identification, capacity strain, and school composition if no new policy is adopted, to serve as a comparator against which active proposals are judged.

implementation_steps.action: Continue existing data collection and publication practices without structural change
Document trajectory of identification counts, workforce vacancies, and segregation complaints under status quo for comparative baseline purposes
implementation_steps.actor: Independent monitoring bodies (e.g. Ombudsman, academic observers)

mechanisms: Continued year-on-year SNI growth concentrated in BTMN-adjacent categories under unchanged definitions and gatekeeping (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01, F-live-legal_and_governance-01, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
Persistent county-level variation (2.6-fold to wider spreads) without reconciliation continues to obscure true drivers (F-live-hungarian_education_system-02, F-live-school_network_planning-03)

Shrinking birth cohorts from 2028 onward interact with an already geographically maldistributed and aging specialist workforce, worsening capacity mismatch without intervention (F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02, F-live-demography-06)

Unresolved ECtHR/EU infringement obligations remain unaddressed, extending legal and reputational exposure indefinitely (F-live-legal_and_governance-09, F-live-political_feasibility-06, F-live-political_feasibility-10)
Diagnostic bottlenecks and post-diagnosis service gaps persist unchanged (F-live-implementation_planning-03, F-live-implementation_planning-04)
risks: Passive drift may allow institutional sorting and segregation mechanisms to deepen further, particularly in small settlements (F-live-school_network_planning-10)
Specialist workforce attrition could outpace enrollment gains in training programs, given uncertain conversion of applicants to practicing teachers (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)
Continued non-compliance with ECtHR judgments risks further EU-level financial or political consequences (F-live-political_feasibility-10)
proposals.system_problem: Current identification, gatekeeping, finance, and placement rules interact with demographic decline, workforce aging, and unresolved segregation litigation without any corrective mechanism; leaving these unchanged produces a foreseeable but undocumented drift path.
proposals.title: Passive baseline: no new SEN policy, observe demographic and administrative drift
uncertainties: Whether the identification trend will plateau, continue linearly, or accelerate absent intervention is not established by any single reconciled series (F-live-implementation_planning-01)
The extent to which passive drift alone would trigger EU financial conditionality specific to education/SEN is unknown (F-live-political_feasibility-10)

assumptions: Administrative capacity exists or can be built to reconcile historically inconsistent data series
Natural-experiment research designs (exploiting 2018/2020 and 2017-18 reforms) can produce sufficiently robust causal estimates given data limitations
proposals.boundary: Bounded strictly to evidence and monitoring architecture — data reconciliation, indicator design, and driver-attribution research; does not change identification criteria, gatekeeping procedures, funding rules, or service delivery.
proposals.change_lever: Statistical and monitoring governance: mandate a single reconciled longitudinal dataset, standardized definitions across levels/regions, and a structured research program testing competing driver hypotheses (prevalence, diagnostic practice, school-entry policy, funding incentives).
costs: Sustained investment in statistical infrastructure and independent research commissioning
Administrative burden on expert committees and schools to standardize reporting formats
proposals.equity_impact: Improved data quality directly supports detecting and eventually addressing Roma misclassification and disadvantaged-child under-identification, both currently obscured by inconsistent statistics (F-live-school_network_planning-09, F-live-legal_and_governance-10); however, measurement alone does not remedy inequity without downstream action.
expected_benefits: Enables evidence-based targeting of subsequent reforms (T3-T6) rather than acting on unverified assumptions about the scale or cause of the identification rise
Provides the data foundation needed to respond credibly to ECtHR and EU Commission requests for evidence on misclassification reduction (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07)
Reduces risk of policy churn driven by contested or unaudited figures (e.g., NGO advocacy estimates in F-live-hungarian_education_system-09)
proposals.goal: Create a single, harmonized, publicly auditable statistical and monitoring architecture that reconciles inconsistent SNI/BTMN series and empirically disentangles prevalence, diagnostic-practice, access, and incentive effects before further policy change is designed.

implementation_steps.action: Design and publish a single reconciled longitudinal SNI/BTMN dataset with consistent definitions across primary, secondary, and cross-level reporting
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education / KSH joint task force

implementation_steps.action: Conduct quasi-experimental driver-attribution studies exploiting the 2018/2020 school-readiness reform and 2017-18 BTMN grading reform as natural experiments
Establish ethnically disaggregated data collection protocol for segregation and misclassification monitoring, subject to legal privacy safeguards
implementation_steps.actor: Ombudsman for Fundamental Rights / statistics authority

mechanisms: Reconcile KSH, ministry, and regional data series into one time-consistent SNI/BTMN dataset spanning at least the last 20 years (F-live-implementation_planning-01, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01)

Use natural policy variation — e.g., the 2018/2020 school-readiness rule change and the 2017-18 BTMN grading-exemption reform — as quasi-experimental tests of driver contribution (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03, F-live-implementation_planning-02)

Track county-level variation systematically to test whether the 2.6-fold-plus spread reflects true prevalence or administrative practice differences, following the Finnish precedent of persistent regional variation post-reform (F-live-hungarian_education_system-02, F-live-finnish_reform-04)

Disaggregate BTMN age-discontinuity (2.1% preschool vs 9.5% school-age) via longitudinal cohort tracking to test screening-sensitivity vs true-emergence hypotheses (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03, F-live-education_finance-04)

Require ethnically disaggregated segregation and misclassification data collection, addressing the documented data gap flagged by UN/Ombudsman sources (F-live-legal_and_governance-10, F-live-hungarian_education_system-07)
risks: Reconciliation exercise may itself become contested if driver-attribution findings threaten institutional or political interests (evidence status contested per F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
Delay risk: if measurement work is used to indefinitely postpone needed capacity or gatekeeping reforms, it could function as a de facto continuation of T1 drift
proposals.system_problem: Multiple inconsistent official statistical series (cross-level vs. primary-only, different baseline years, conflicting autism counts) prevent policymakers from knowing whether, how much, and why identification has risen, undermining any downstream policy design (F-live-implementation_planning-01, F-live-education_finance-02, F-live-portuguese_reform-01).
proposals.title: Establish a reconciled national SEN measurement and driver-attribution system
uncertainties: Whether reconciled data will resolve or merely reframe the contested dispute between official 'diagnostic modernization' explanations and independent experts' school-entry-age explanation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
Feasibility and political acceptability of collecting ethnically disaggregated data given Hungary's documented reluctance to date (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)

assumptions: Hungary's legal and administrative system can absorb a tiered-model pilot without the scale of disruption seen in Poland's rapid, non-consensual 2016-17 reform
Comparative evidence from Wales/Finland is sufficiently transferable to Hungary's distinct legal-administrative context to inform design, subject to explicit testing rather than direct import
proposals.boundary: Bounded to how identification is defined and adjudicated — category boundaries, gatekeeping procedure, and appeal/review timelines; explicitly excludes funding formula redesign (T4) and workforce capacity building (T5), which are separate levers even though related.
proposals.change_lever: Definitional and gatekeeping redesign: consider a non-categorical or tiered support model (as in Wales' ALN framework or Finland's three-tier system) that decouples formal labeling from support access, alongside gatekeeping capacity reform to reduce waiting lists.
costs: Legal amendment costs and transition-period administrative complexity
Retraining of expert-committee staff and school administrators on new tiered criteria
Risk of short-term confusion during transition, as documented in Poland's rapid, contested 2016-17 reform (F-live-polish_reform-06, F-live-polish_reform-07)
proposals.equity_impact: A tiered model could reduce the sharp legal discontinuities (e.g., BTMN grading-exemption cliff) that may drive reclassification, but comparative evidence (Finland) shows persistent regional variation after reform, and without parallel action on Roma misclassification (addressed separately in T6), gatekeeping redesign alone may not resolve ethnic-disproportionality concerns (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07).
expected_benefits: Reduced diagnostic bottleneck and waiting times by decoupling support access from full formal determination for lower-severity cases
Reduced category-boundary gaming (e.g., BTMN-to-SNI reclassification incentives) by removing sharp legal discontinuities between adjacent categories
Comparative evidence suggests potential reduction in headline identification inflation driven by definitional architecture rather than true need
proposals.goal: Restructure the definitional and diagnostic architecture — the SNI/BTMN boundary and single-gatekeeper expert-committee system — to reduce arbitrary category-driven variation and diagnostic bottlenecks, informed by transferability-tested comparative reforms.

implementation_steps.action: Commission a formal transferability assessment of tiered/non-categorical models (Wales ALN, Finland three-tier) against Hungary's legal, fiscal, and workforce context
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education, in consultation with pedagógiai szakszolgálat leadership

implementation_steps.action: Pilot a tiered support framework in a subset of counties, retaining SNI/BTMN legal categories for funding purposes but decoupling day-to-day support access from full expert-committee determination
implementation_steps.actor: Legislature (amending Act CXC of 2011 and EMMI decree 15/2013)
implementation_steps.timeline: pilot in years 2-3, national rollout decision at year 4

implementation_steps.action: Reform appeal/review timelines and reduce waiting-list bottlenecks through triage protocols separating urgent from routine assessments

mechanisms: Wales' shift to non-categorical Additional Learning Needs framework produced measurably lower identification (11.2% vs 13.6-37% in comparator UK nations), demonstrating identification-rate sensitivity to definitional architecture — offered as transferability-tested comparative evidence, not an endorsed template (F-live-international_comparison-06)

Finland's tiered model decouples formal SEN identification from support access (31% receiving some tier of support without mandatory labeling), suggesting an alternative to Hungary's current binary label-gated system (F-live-international_comparison-07, F-live-finnish_reform-01)

However, Finnish post-reform experience shows growth simply relocated to a new intermediate tier (2.8%→12.2% in 'intensified support') rather than being eliminated, and regional variation (4%-13%) persisted post-reform — indicating tiering redesigns identification patterns but does not resolve underlying variation or gatekeeping capacity (F-live-finnish_reform-02, F-live-finnish_reform-04)

The 2017-18 Hungarian reform removing BTMN grading exemption (while retaining it for SNI) created a documented reclassification incentive toward the more restrictive SNI category, illustrating how category-boundary rules alone can move numbers independent of clinical need (F-live-legal_and_governance-03, F-live-implementation_planning-02)

Hungary's single-tier county/district-level gatekeeping structure (SNI at county level, BTMN at district level, neither established by medical diagnosis alone) has not been restructured despite rising caseloads and waiting lists of several months (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-04)

Poland's centralization of diagnostic authority in Psychological and Pedagogical Counselling Centres, separate from schools, offers a comparator gatekeeping structure, though Poland's own reform was implemented without consensus-building and produced significant transition disruption (F-live-polish_reform-05, F-live-polish_reform-06)
risks: Poland's experience shows rapid structural reform without consensus-building can produce major implementation disruption (double-cohort crunch, teacher strikes) (F-live-polish_reform-07, F-live-polish_reform-09)

Finland's experience shows tiering may relocate rather than resolve identification growth and regional variation, limiting expected benefit magnitude (F-live-finnish_reform-02, F-live-finnish_reform-04)
Only 23% of Finland's top-tier students are fully mainstream-included despite the tiered model, suggesting tiering alone does not guarantee integration outcomes (F-live-finnish_reform-05)

Hungary's classroom structure (24 students, one generalist teacher, one specialist across four years) lacks the co-teaching infrastructure central to Finland's tiered implementation, raising transferability doubts absent parallel capacity investment (F-live-international_comparison-10)
proposals.system_problem: A binary SNI/BTMN legal categorization adjudicated solely by a single nationwide expert-committee gatekeeper with multi-month waiting lists produces both diagnostic bottlenecks and category-boundary effects (e.g., the 2017-18 reform's incentive to shift BTMN cases toward SNI) that are structurally, not clinically, driven (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-03, F-live-implementation_planning-02).
proposals.title: Redesign SNI/BTMN category boundaries and expert-committee gatekeeping toward a tiered, needs-based model
uncertainties: Whether decoupling support access from formal SNI/BTMN determination is legally and fiscally feasible given Hungary's funding architecture is tied to classification (interacts with T4)
Whether a pilot approach can generate valid comparative results given regional variation is already extreme and may confound pilot vs. non-pilot comparisons (F-live-school_network_planning-03)

assumptions: An audit can reliably distinguish the effect of finance design from other co-occurring drivers (diagnostic practice, school-entry rules) on Hungary's identification trend
Census-based allocation can be designed with sufficient needs-sensitivity to avoid under-serving genuinely high-need children
proposals.boundary: Bounded strictly to funding-formula mechanics — per-pupil weighting, normative multipliers, census- vs diagnosis-gated allocation; explicitly excludes changes to diagnostic criteria/gatekeeping (T3) and to workforce capacity delivery (T5).
proposals.change_lever: Finance-design reform: move toward census-based or block-grant allocation (as in Finland's 2010 shift) or restructure diagnosis-linked multipliers to reduce the marginal fiscal return on additional formal identifications, informed by comparative evidence on funding-identification linkage.
costs: Risk of short-term revenue disruption for schools currently reliant on SNI-linked multipliers, particularly small rural schools already fiscally squeezed
Administrative cost of formula redesign and transition monitoring
proposals.equity_impact: Poorly calibrated finance reform risks disproportionately harming under-resourced rural and disadvantaged-area schools already facing fiscal squeeze (F-live-demography-09), unless the census-based component is paired with equity-weighted base funding for disadvantaged regions; conversely, removing perverse incentives could reduce incentive-driven over-classification of Roma or disadvantaged children documented elsewhere in the evidence.
expected_benefits: Potential reduction in financially-driven over-identification if audit confirms current multipliers create such incentives
Alignment with comparative evidence (Finland, England, Poland) on the sensitivity of identification counts to funding architecture
Improved fiscal predictability for local governments facing demographic-driven enrollment decline (relevant given F-live-demography-09 fiscal squeeze)
proposals.goal: Reduce financial incentives embedded in per-pupil normative funding multipliers that may reward formal classification independent of actual service need, replacing or supplementing them with allocation mechanisms less sensitive to individual diagnosis counts.

implementation_steps.action: Audit current per-pupil multiplier structure against actual per-pupil service cost to determine whether current incentives favor over- or under-identification, informed by T2's driver-attribution findings
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Education joint working group
implementation_steps.timeline: 12 months, sequenced after or parallel to T2 initial findings

implementation_steps.action: Pilot a partially census-based allocation component for a subset of local government education budgets, retaining a needs-tiered supplement for verified high-cost cases

Monitor whether finance-formula changes produce unintended reductions in service availability for genuinely high-need children, given documented risk that current funding already under-resources actual need

mechanisms: Non-census-based special education funding correlates with increased SEN provision; Finland's 2010 shift to census-based allocation reduced full-time special-education placement, offered as comparative (not directly causal for Hungary) evidence of the funding-identification link (F-live-finnish_reform-03)

England's IFS analysis identifies ring-fenced funding blocks, frozen thresholds, and EHCP-gated top-up funding as plausible drivers of a 71% rise in awards, and regions with higher special-education funding show more segregated placements — indicating funding design, not just need, shapes identification and placement patterns (F-live-international_comparison-04, F-live-international_comparison-05)

Poland's 2025 shift to a disability-weighted per-pupil algorithm shows funding levels can rise independent of prevalence change (14% subvention increase 2023-24 unrelated to prevalence), illustrating that finance reform itself can be decoupled from identification counts if designed deliberately (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-03)

Hungary's SNI multiplier (2-3x pupil-equivalent for subsidy) has plausible but unproven incentive effects on identification; existing evidence is explicitly correlational/theoretical, not causally established for the current Hungarian rise (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)

Current evidence also suggests financing has become insufficiently generous to actually support extra resources, meaning some schools now avoid rather than seek SNI-classified pupils — implying the incentive direction may have already reversed and requires diagnosis before redesign (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
risks: Evidence status for Hungary's own SNI multiplier's causal effect on identification is weak/theoretical (F-live-legal_and_governance-07); acting without T2 verification risks addressing an unconfirmed mechanism

Given equity_and_social_mobility-04 suggests financing may already be insufficient to support extra resources, a census-based shift without added funding could worsen access for genuinely high-need children rather than merely removing perverse incentives

England's evidence shows funding-linked allocation is associated with both awards and segregated placement rates — a poorly designed formula change could inadvertently affect placement patterns addressed separately under T6
proposals.system_problem: Hungary's task-based financing ties per-pupil subsidy directly to SNI/BTMN classification (pupils counted as 2-3 for subsidy purposes), and historically pre-2013 higher per-capita funding for SNI students created documented institutional incentives to register students, while more recent reports suggest classification has become exclusionary rather than resource-enabling as financing fails to cover actual costs (F-live-legal_and_governance-07, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04, F-live-education_finance-08).
proposals.title: Shift SNI/BTMN per-pupil financing from diagnosis-gated multipliers toward census-based or needs-tiered allocation
uncertainties: Whether Hungary's specific multiplier structure has in fact driven measurable over-identification, or whether — as equity_and_social_mobility-04 suggests — classification has become a net cost/exclusionary factor rather than an incentive, reversing the expected direction of reform effect
Sequencing dependency: this proposal's evidence base is materially strengthened or weakened by T2's driver-attribution findings, which are not yet available

assumptions: Regional financial incentives can meaningfully alter specialist location choices despite broader national teacher-shortage and low-pay conditions
Completion-to-practice tracking is administratively feasible to implement across training institutions
proposals.boundary: Bounded to workforce supply, geographic distribution, and support-delivery capacity (assessment throughput and post-diagnosis service provision); explicitly excludes changes to identification criteria (T3) and funding-formula design (T4), though it depends on adequate finance to be viable.
proposals.change_lever: Workforce and service-delivery capacity building: targeted regional recruitment incentives, co-teaching infrastructure investment, triage-based assessment capacity expansion, and monitoring of post-diagnosis service delivery compliance.
costs: Sustained public investment in recruitment incentives, salary premiums, and training-pipeline expansion
Administrative cost of building new compliance-monitoring and triage systems
proposals.equity_impact: Directly targets the documented regional inequity in specialist access (Northeast Hungary had no training program until 2017; long-standing weak services and waiting lists there), which disproportionately affects disadvantaged and Roma-concentrated regions (F-live-school_network_planning-05, F-live-demography-10); improved assess-to-serve delivery also addresses the documented pattern that multiply disadvantaged children are least likely to have needs identified or served (F-live-school_network_planning-09).
expected_benefits: Closes documented gap between formal identification and actual service receipt, directly addressing UN Special Rapporteur concerns (F-live-portuguese_reform-04)
Regional targeting addresses the most acute geographic maldistribution rather than uniform national measures that would underserve worst-affected areas
Improved workforce data (completion-to-practice tracking) creates better planning basis than current enrollment-count proxies
proposals.goal: Close the gap between rising formal identification and actual service delivery by expanding and redistributing the special-education specialist workforce, reducing diagnostic bottlenecks, and closing the assess-to-serve implementation gap.

implementation_steps.action: Design region-differentiated recruitment and retention incentive packages (e.g. housing, loan forgiveness) targeted at northeastern and rural counties with documented specialist deficits
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education / Ministry of Human Capacities

implementation_steps.action: Establish tracked completion-to-practice pathways distinguishing career-track students from correspondence-program parent-students, reporting practicing-workforce conversion rates annually
Implement triage protocols to separate urgent from routine assessments and rebalance specialist time allocation away from paperwork toward direct service, building on 2013 centralization lessons
Establish systematic monitoring and public reporting of post-diagnosis habilitation/therapy-hour delivery compliance rates by region
implementation_steps.actor: School inspectorate / Ministry of Education compliance unit

mechanisms: Specialist teacher shortage is most acute in Budapest (45 unfilled posts) and rural Transdanubian/northeastern counties (2-3 posts, some districts with none), requiring differentiated regional incentive design rather than uniform national policy (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05, F-live-legal_and_governance-08, F-live-school_network_planning-05)

Rising applications to special-education degree programs (15% increase to 3,768 in 2026) do not reliably convert to practicing workforce supply, since many applicants are parents of affected children in correspondence programs unlikely to enter the profession — indicating recruitment marketing alone is insufficient; retention and completion-to-practice pathways must be separately targeted (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05, F-live-education_finance-07)

Diagnostic bottlenecks (multi-month waiting lists, staff turnover, insufficient medical backup) constitute a documented capacity constraint distinct from workforce numbers alone, requiring triage-protocol and administrative-capacity investment (F-live-implementation_planning-03)

Post-diagnosis service delivery failure — children not receiving legally prescribed habilitation/therapy hours — represents a second-stage implementation gap requiring compliance monitoring separate from assessment capacity (F-live-implementation_planning-04)

2013 centralization of counseling services shifted specialist time toward assessment/paperwork and away from therapy provision, an organizational-design driver of capacity misallocation that could be reversed through workload rebalancing (F-live-implementation_planning-05, F-live-education_finance-06)

Finland's co-teaching and dual-teacher infrastructure, absent in Hungary's typical 24-student single-generalist-plus-annual-specialist classroom model, is identified as a structural gap relevant to any inclusion-capacity investment (F-live-international_comparison-10)

UN Special Rapporteur assessment corroborates that growing SEN cohorts are not matched by adequate teacher training or specialist support nationally (F-live-portuguese_reform-04, F-live-demography-07)
risks: Regional incentive packages may be insufficient to overcome broader structural drivers of specialist flight from disadvantaged areas (documented aging and low-pay context) (F-live-demography-06, F-live-portuguese_reform-03)

Recent 2023 unified teacher legal-status law restricting strike rights and enabling unilateral reassignment could be used to address shortages coercively rather than through incentive-based recruitment, creating labor-relations risk (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
Workforce expansion without corresponding finance reform (T4) or gatekeeping reform (T3) may simply increase assessment throughput without resolving underlying identification-driver ambiguity
proposals.system_problem: Rising SEN caseloads are met with an aging, geographically maldistributed specialist workforce (45 unfilled Budapest positions vs. 2-3 in rural counties; several northeastern districts with none), multi-month diagnostic waiting lists, and documented post-diagnosis failure to deliver legally prescribed habilitation/therapy hours — a capacity problem distinct from and downstream of identification rules (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05, F-live-legal_and_governance-08, F-live-implementation_planning-04).
proposals.title: Build regionally targeted specialist workforce and assess-to-serve delivery capacity
uncertainties: Whether recruitment incentives can overcome the documented disconnect between rising applications and actual practicing-workforce entry (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05, F-live-education_finance-07)
Whether the 2025 salary reform (21.2% raise) meaningfully affects specialist (as opposed to generalist) recruitment, since no source quantifies this distinction (F-live-portuguese_reform-03)

assumptions: Political will exists or can be generated to extend anti-segregation obligations into the politically sensitive domain of free school choice and selective tracks
EU Commission and Council of Europe monitoring mechanisms retain sufficient leverage to incentivize compliance
proposals.boundary: Bounded to compositional-sorting mechanisms and anti-segregation legal obligations — school choice, admissions discretion, and classification-driven ethnic sorting; does not itself redesign SEN diagnostic categories (T3) or funding formulas (T4), though it draws on findings from both where they intersect with segregation.
proposals.change_lever: Legal-accountability and choice-architecture reform: extend anti-segregation obligations to cover school-choice admissions discretion and selective tracks, implement ECtHR/EU-mandated safeguards against misclassification, and require ethnically disaggregated segregation monitoring.
costs: Political cost of restricting free school choice or selective-track autonomy, likely to face resistance from schools and some parent constituencies
Administrative cost of audit and disaggregated-data infrastructure
proposals.equity_impact: This proposal is centrally an equity intervention: it directly targets documented Roma over-classification, discriminatory admissions exclusion, and the specific policy gap (choice and selective tracks) left unaddressed by existing anti-segregation law, aiming to fulfil outstanding ECtHR and EU Commission-mandated non-discrimination obligations (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07, F-live-legal_and_governance-09, F-live-legal_and_governance-10).
expected_benefits: Progress toward resolving decade-plus outstanding ECtHR judgments and EU Commission infringement procedure, reducing legal and reputational exposure and potential future funding conditionality risk (F-live-political_feasibility-10)
Closes the specific structural gap left open by the 2023 anti-segregation law, addressing school-choice and selective-track channels that operate independent of SEN policy
Improved data enables distinguishing SEN-classification-driven sorting from other segregation channels, informing more precise future intervention
proposals.goal: Address how SEN/BTMN classification, free school choice, and institutional selectivity interact to reshape school ethnic and socioeconomic composition, and bring Hungary into compliance with outstanding ECtHR and EU Commission obligations on Roma segregation.

implementation_steps.action: Submit the previously withheld data on whether revised diagnostic procedures have reduced Roma misclassification, and develop a compliance roadmap with Committee of Ministers oversight
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Justice / Ministry of Education, in response to ECtHR/EU Commission obligations
implementation_steps.timeline: 12 months, treated as an overdue legal obligation rather than discretionary policy choice

implementation_steps.action: Extend the 2023 anti-segregation law's scope to cover discretionary out-of-catchment admissions criteria and establish transparency/audit requirements for selective gimnázium track admissions
Implement systematic ethnically disaggregated segregation and misclassification monitoring, coordinated with T2's broader measurement reform
Establish independent audit of school-level SNI-intake self-limitation practices (documented as informal, uncentralized) to determine whether they function as exclusionary sorting

mechanisms: The 2023 anti-segregation law's scope gap — leaving free choice under Act Nkt. and selective gimnázium tracks untouched — is documented as a structural channel for segregation independent of SEN-specific policy (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06)

Kertesi-Kézdi research establishes that school-switching behavior, driven by free choice, significantly alters school ethnic composition and is more predictive of segregation than residential distribution alone, and that segregation is most strongly predicted by town-level Roma proportion (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08, F-live-implementation_planning-08)

Documented threshold effect: in schools with 50-75% Roma pupils, children are more likely to be tested and referred to segregated SEN classes, while above 75% Roma concentration, separation may occur outside the SEN system entirely — indicating SEN classification is one but not the only segregation channel requiring monitoring beyond SEN-specific rules (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07, F-live-portuguese_reform-05)

Discretionary school admission authority has been documented as exercised to exclude Roma students seeking transfer to non-segregated schools, a mechanism operating through choice/admissions rather than SEN classification (F-live-political_feasibility-08)

Hungary has decade-plus unresolved ECtHR (Horváth and Kiss 2013, Szolcsán 2023) and EU Commission (2016 infringement) findings on discriminatory misclassification and segregation, with the government not providing requested data on whether new diagnostic procedures reduced Roma misclassification (F-live-legal_and_governance-09, F-live-hungarian_education_system-07, F-live-political_feasibility-09)

Ombudsman data show segregation complaints constituted 69% of Fundamental Rights complaints in 2023, and Hungary does not systematically collect ethnically disaggregated segregation data, impeding accountability (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)

Rising private/church school growth, driven partly by middle-class flight from deteriorating public schools, is flagged by UN assessment as a mechanism that will concentrate disadvantage in public schools, compounding SEN-related sorting dynamics (F-live-portuguese_reform-09)
risks: Given the government's documented non-compliance history (withheld data, open infringement procedure since 2016), political will to extend legal obligations to choice/admissions systems is uncertain (F-live-political_feasibility-09, F-live-legal_and_governance-09)

EU Commission has not deployed sector-specific SEN/education conditionality despite the open infringement procedure, suggesting external leverage may be limited without additional pressure (F-live-political_feasibility-10)
Restricting discretionary admissions could face legal or political challenge from school autonomy advocates
proposals.system_problem: The December 2023 anti-segregation law leaves free parental school choice under voucher financing and selective gimnázium tracks untouched, while documented mechanisms — Roma over-classification thresholds, discretionary out-of-catchment admissions used to exclude transferring Roma students, and unresolved ECtHR/EU Commission findings — continue to drive compositional sorting independent of, and interacting with, SEN identification (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06, F-live-political_feasibility-08, F-live-legal_and_governance-09).
proposals.title: Strengthen anti-segregation accountability and close school-choice sorting channels interacting with SEN classification
uncertainties: Whether extending legal accountability to choice/admissions systems is achievable given the government's decade-long pattern of non-compliance and data withholding

Whether closing the SEN-classification segregation channel alone (per the 50-75% Roma threshold finding) would meaningfully reduce overall segregation given that above 75% Roma concentration, separation appears to occur through other channels entirely (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
